quotes.quote: As my old boss told me, 'if any number is wrong, they're all wrong.'
Ultimately, making data believable means having empathy for the stakeholder and knowledge of the domain and context in which they will use data for decision-making.
During the good times of 2020/21, data teams could coast by on this approach, with future promises of providing value 'someday.' Those days are long gone.
The grim reaper of cost-cutting will spare data teams that add value and cull those that do not.

Ignore the big picture, work in a silo and completely ignore your stakeholders. Instead, be inwardly focused, overcomplicate everything, and obsess over the hottest data tools and fads. That's how it went in the good times.
You know simplicity when you see it. Simplicity means designing things with the fewest moving parts, making each part easy to understand and work with.
My litmus test for simplicity in data engineering - Is there anything that can be removed without sacrificing your needed form and function?
Sadly, data engineers are often addicted to complexity. It tempts a data engineer to engineer things, sometimes sacrificing simplicity.
There's no extra credit for unnecessary complexity. Always strive for simplicity.
Sarah Catanzaro is a general partner at Amplify Partners, which invested in dbt, Motherduck, and many other high-profile data and technology companies.
Abundance has a way of distracting people into the hallucination of progress. On the other hand, constraints and scarcity have a way of sharpening the mind.
With direction elusive, often data teams were quite a bit like performative theater, doing experimental 'data stuff' but not contributing to business impact.
If your data team was removed, would the business be negatively impacted? If the answer is no, you need to start looking for a new job since you're probably not the first to ask this question.
In the good old days, data teams had to figure out how to grow at all costs. These data teams need to figure out how to survive at all costs.
When I speak with Zhamak about her vision for Data Mesh, it comes from her dystopian fear that big companies and governments control access to data. In the end, data needs to be free.
Fast forward to today, and the Internet is mostly a cesspool controlled by large monopolies.
The equivalent of Napster is now possible for data products.
Data Mesh is also about avoiding any single monolithic product or vendor, as this defeats the purpose of decentralization.
Systems, habits, and 'grinding it out' every day are how you get wherever you want.
Few things in life come easy, and everything takes longer than you think.
Success is often the result of many years of simply improving yourself and avoiding too many stupid things.
Several years from now, you'll look back with awe at how much skill, knowledge, and wisdom you've accumulated by simply doing the same boring thing over and over.

Five years ago, some companies made huge investments in AI without having enough high-quality foundational data to train and run the algorithms. That left executives underwhelmed by the results and disillusioned.
I still remember people claiming BI, SQL, and data warehousing were dead in the water because AI would make them obsolete in only a few years.

Vendors would oversell the AI capabilities of their products, often claiming to be doing AI when a quick look under the hood revealed a bunch of if-else statements, linear regression, or something similarly unsophisticated.
Even early on, I realized that ML algorithms were relatively easy compared to challenges such as scoping and framing the problem, getting a good dataset for training, hosting models, retraining, etc.

Succeeding with a data initiative is hard work. It requires building a solid foundation of robust data architecture and systems, getting good at the basics before you leap into AI, and fostering a data-friendly culture.
The recent layoffs are a reminder of the Golden Rule - whoever holds the gold makes the rules.
It's common to see data professionals granted a lot of responsibility but little to no power to take action on the insights from the data they produce.
Not all value is created equal. Value is transitive.
You and your team need to be in violent agreement with the business about what value means, how it is measured, and who is responsible for delivering it.
Your true north is knowing your ultimate customer and what they value.
It involves a ton of hard work that you'll probably not want to do, and an insane amount of luck that you'll falsely attribute to your not-so-hard work.
I'd like a place to rant on a regular basis, either screaming at the sky or rambling with my friends.
I'll be giving very candid takes on the technology and data industry. Lord knows we need it.
Zach Wilson is a force of nature in the data engineering space, with a massive following and candid advice about working as a data engineer, and the challenges of mental health.
The business doesn't care about data. The business cares about making money.
When I ask for the time, don't tell me how to make a watch. Just tell me the time.
Data is just a means to an end. As a data professional, the sooner you realize this, the longer and more successful you'll be in this industry.
Stop leading with data in your discussions with the business. Learn and speak the language of your stakeholders.
The audience was largely a non-data audience, comprised mostly of DevOps and software engineers. Talk about a tough crowd!

What made sense for the start-up no longer works at scale. When applications require trust, like AI/ML pipelines, data products, or financial reporting, the flexibility of the Modern Data Stack and its democratization suddenly becomes a massive liability.
Anyone who thinks this is an altruistic endeavor hasn't paid attention. In other news, water is wet.
When in doubt, RTFM.

For data professionals, I see True North as data needs to be as accurate and believable as possible, delivered as quickly and seamlessly as possible, promoting the best data-driven decisions and outcomes.

First principles mean getting back to basics. It means understanding how things work at an atomic level. This takes a ton of work, and there are no shortcuts if you want to call yourself an expert in something.
If you read Through the Looking Glass, you'll learn about the Red Queen Effect, where you run harder and harder to stay in the same place. Avoid this treadmill trap.
Knowledge compounds. But, it happens very slowly, similar to compound interest. You need to be patient.
Engineering throws stuff to the data team, expecting the data team to deal with it. As the old saying goes, shit flows downhill, in this case, to the data teams.
Data is the application.
The ubiquity of ML/AI, streaming data, reverse ETL (I wish this term would disappear), and other feedback loops back to the application mean data isn't something that lives outside the application.
Inevitably, the software and data divide will disappear.

Get off your stupid phones and social media and interact with people in real life, consider alternatives to college (I'm a huge fan of apprenticeships), find a career that will be hard to automate, and learn to work and live alongside AI.

Lately, I've been chatting with people about how the rush to AI might (key word - might) be what companies need to take data quality seriously. Of course, I've been through a few other periods of ML/AI excitement, and it didn't happen then. So who the hell knows?
Or, make up a bunch of shit like a lot of 'AI' companies right now. Price the 'AI' using ChatGPT, an abacus, and a magic 8-Ball.
We are not modeling reality, but the way information about reality is processed, by people.
The lack of a data model is still a data model, albeit a potentially crappy one.
Ironically, in a field that advocates for consistent data governance and management, we struggle to use a simple word like 'model' consistently or accurately.
The nature of programming has already changed. That said, reasoning about code won't go away anytime soon. You still need to know if and why AI-generated code works.
Same grift, different day. Seems like everyone is suddenly an 'AI' expert.
Most companies are still barely doing BI, and now they want to do AI. This won't work.
A semantic layer is, at best, a bandaid that doesn't solve the core problem that a lot of upstream data you depend upon is built on incredibly shaky ground these days.

To all the purists out there who've been arguing for decades that your way is the one true way of data modeling - your tirades and idiotic behavior are holding the industry back. If your approach had worked by now, we wouldn't be in this mess.
If we can't get data modeling right, we're in for a world of hurt.
It felt like somebody forced a wedge in the middle of the data community, and it made for awkward and expensive decisions for sponsors and attendees.
The major data platforms are in an arms race, which is expected behavior. Kill or be killed.
We get the community we deserve. We deserve better.

One of these conferences asked me to give a talk about products they want to push. On top of that, timelines were super aggressive for slides to be submitted 'because legal review,' AND they weren't comping my speaking fee and travel. Since I'm not a charity, don't enter into lopsided relationships, and I'm in the middle of many paid projects, I decided this wasn't a good fit for me.
Your technical chops are table stakes and probably account for a small fraction of your overall success.
If you want to level up in your career past most people, it's time to learn people skills.
Your ability to communicate, relate, empathize, and help others get to where they want to go will differentiate you from 99.999% of that pack.
You have the personality of a paper plate, but that's good enough.
The number of people I see 'working in the cloud' but lacking knowledge of how to work in the cloud properly is pretty appalling.
Universally, when I talk with anyone who handles data (developers, 'data people,' etc.), data modeling is forgotten, ignored, and sometimes scoffed at as being 'too difficult and slow.'

The notion is to react to the question at hand, then move on to the next query…and the next query…and the next query…Sort of like how a puppy gets excited about the world, including pooping all over your nice rug.
Apparently, companies that eschew data modeling perform just fine. They make a ton of money, certainly enough to throw costly compute to crunch whatever query needs to be run.
What if we just forget the old practices and techniques of data modeling ever existed? Would we be fine?
A lack of a data model is still a data model, albeit a crappy one. And users can tell and smell the consequences of this. It smells like a NYC dumpster on a fine summer afternoon.

To say Kimball is irrelevant because of columnar storage and unlimited compute is a dangerously ignorant statement. It is like saying because cars have more horsepower, you don't need to know how to drive a car.
The disturbing trend I've seen is that as we replace data modeling with fancy tools, we're drifting from our ability to think and reason about data.
To completely ignore data modeling is professionally negligent, and I'll argue you're unfit for your job.
Many data practitioners put performance ahead of all else. This is exactly backward.
Companies and people say they're 'data-driven.' This is a nice platitude, and often furthest from the politics that actually drive organizational behavior.
The ironic thing is while you can practice Agile all you want, your boss's boss already decided what she wants to do, your boss decided what he wants to do, and so on.
Know that technical excellence and 'data' are not what drive most people. What drives most people are ego-driven - climbing the career ladder, outshining others, and money.
Know the reasons behind the reasons and the questions behind the questions.
The biggest issue in the data industry today is the massive knowledge gap about the basics of data and best practices for the tools we use.
We have all of these fancy toys, but from what I've seen, we're often not using them to their fullest capabilities.
If users don't take the time to educate themselves on the best practices for the tools they use, is that the vendor's fault?
As the old saying goes, Read The F*cking Manual.

One dev put it perfectly - he'd probably help the data people at his company if 1) the data people were crystal clear on what they wanted from him and 2) he had the incentive and bandwidth to help. But since he had no time or incentive to help anyone but his immediate team, the requests from the data team were ignored.
Data is the application.

I urge data teams to build a bridge with the dev teams they depend upon. It doesn't need to be fancy. Do lunch and learns with the dev team. Educate them on what you do, and why it matters to the company. Simple things like this will build empathy, and a little empathy goes a long ways.
The world has moved on from data teams providing basic reporting. It did that a while ago.
Anyone who's seen these systems knows the horror show that often awaits. We often ignore the 'boring' stuff like data modeling, quality, and governance.
But if the underlying data the LLM is built upon is crap, given LLM's tendency to hallucinate on a good day, what do you think will happen?
With the massive attention on AI, this is THE opportunity for the data industry to start leading from the front.
If we can't take stuff like data modeling, quality, and governance seriously now, when will we?
No matter where you go in the data world, the problems are the same.
Too often, data practitioners are far removed from the core parts of the business. Often being on the receiving end, they're playing defense by default.
Sh*t flows downhill, and data teams are famous for their ability to receive sh*t in volumes, often by the truckload.
If you can show that you care about the business, the business will care about you. Respect gets respect.
If Not Now, When?
Boring is Back! Solving the classical data problems is getting simpler, and this allows us to move up the value chain to solve 'boring' problems.
I sometimes joke we're in a 'classical ML/deep learning winter.'
Lots of dog shit companies out there about to face a day of reckoning.
Tim wasn't delivering software; Tim was delivering a team that was delivering software.

Boring is back. Because the focus isn't on managing the underlying technology anymore, we can now turn our attention to the boring stuff we conveniently ignored during the heyday of the modern data stack era - data governance, data management, data modeling, and other 'enterprisey' practices.
I saw first-hand how most corporate datasets were very messy, lacked sufficient quality, and barely worked for BI, let alone AI. The rise of LLM's poured gasoline on this hot mess.
It's like we forgot decades of formal data modeling practices because it's easier to just throw compute at the problem. Turns out, the problem doesn't simply disappear.
It's not just about data anymore, it's about turning data into knowledge that's believable and useful for humans and machines.
The most significant gap in our industry isn't tooling... we need to level up our skills and knowledge to leverage the incredible power at our fingertips properly.
To paraphrase Charlie Munger, without foundational understanding, 'you are like a one-legged man in an ass-kicking contest.'
Skip the step of understanding how servers, networking, storage, and distributed computing work, and you're dead in the water.
At a minimum, you'll duct tape over problems, incurring all forms of debt at high interest rates that'll need to be paid back.
It's not tooling that holds us back. The big challenge is the mastery, knowledge, and understanding of how to use these tools from first principles.
It seems like every company embarks on data engineering on its own, and it's a cold-start problem every single time.
My big question is, if data engineering is to grow as a profession, why don't we have a way to help standardize our practices?
I've traveled the globe, and I have a good sense of where data engineering is on a macro level. We've got a lot of work to do.
For data engineering to mature as a profession, we'll need to start thinking globally.
Most 'AI companies' I've seen are the modern-day equivalent - opportunistic carnival barkers hawking AI solutions to organizations who often don't understand what they're buying.
AI is an accelerator of good existing data hygiene and practices, not a replacement for it.
Your investment into AI will rarely leave the POC stage, if it even gets there.
WTF is this, fantasy land?
I stick to Warren Buffett's approach of 'praise by name, criticize by category.'
No matter where I go in the world, the question of 'What tools should I use?' pops up repeatedly.
Data teams often choose the path that doesn't work, jumping straight to #2. This is a massive mistake; inevitably, many things are built that nobody asked for and don't add value.
Business case, then tools. Never the reverse.

Tooling discussions usually devolve into a pissing match of how much trivia everyone knows about particular tools. While those conversations are fun occasionally, they're highly distracting and usually a waste of time.
If CEOs are this disconnected from marketing - a field that's been around for decades - how connected do you think a CEO is to 'data,' which is comparatively a brand-spanking new field?
Everyone wants to own the data but not be accountable for improving it.
The only job with a shorter tenure than CMO is the CDO. Data is hard on a good day, and CDOs are often set up for failure.
CEOs are pressuring CIOs (and similar roles) to deliver on AI, whatever the hell that means.
The track record of data management's pursuit of perfection is beyond poor.
Perfect datasets very rarely exist. I'm not even sure what a perfect dataset looks like, but I do know what useful datasets look like.
Data management's ideal of 'grown-ups in the room' won't work anymore (it barely worked in the past). Unscalable problems for humans are perfect use cases for machine learning and AI.
Chain together various purpose-built data management AI agents, and you've got an army of data management 'professionals' working tirelessly on managing all of your data assets.

If you predicted the exact outcome of the events of OpenAI last week, please make this public. Could you prove you knew every tiny twist and turn as it happened, second by second? Otherwise (at least to me), you're a charlatan and bullshit artist.
'I don't know' are often the best three words you can say in situations of major uncertainty. Because you often don't know. Simple as that.
It's like dealing with an employee (at best, an intern) who does mundane work and oscillates between decent and poor performance on an hourly basis.
I'd rather focus on running an actual business that does weird things like serving customers who pay money and making a profit.
In Data LARPs, data teams produce very little that the business uses. Often, the data team knows this.
POCs feel like work, but they're just toys.
Especially with the hype around AI, there will be a ton of Data LARPing. Every company wants to get into AI. Very few will get anywhere. Same as it ever was.
If you spend a lot of time working on stuff nobody uses, you're probably LARPing.
Life is too short.
It's like the people who understand data know it's difficult and want to get the basics right, and the ones pushing AI are doing it for…other reasons.
Not having an 'AI Strategy' is akin to not having a website. 'WTF…where's the AI?' screams a CEO who might not even understand what the A and the I stand for.
The chasm between AI overlords running an organization and the Excel-hell in most organizations is very wide.

There's a notion that AI will compensate for human deficiencies and 'make the world a better place,' essentially saving us from ourselves. I'm very bullish on the potential of AI, but I disagree that our AI overlords will save us from ourselves.
Focus on your data strategy before your AI strategy. But these days, companies often want to jump straight into AI without any strategy.
Unexpected events trump linear incrementalism. By definition, you can't predict these unexpected events.
Most predictions I see are linear and incremental extrapolations of the present into the future. That works until it doesn't.

In January 2022, it would be easy to assume the Modern Data Stack and Data Mesh would still reign supreme in 2023. Then in November 2022, ChatGPT ushered in the 'year of AI' in 2023, where every modern data stack vendor is suddenly an AI company.
I find that if I can improve the foundation of my mind and health and bring my best self to each day, it's much easier to execute goals, no matter when they pop up or what they are.
2023 was about the hype of AI. 2024 will be about where AI is a useful value-add.

Just because you're doing AI doesn't mean you're exempt from the age-old problems startups must overcome - solving legitimate business problems, a great customer experience, product-market fit, and getting paying customers.
LLM powered chatbots are cute, and I don't find them very a massively transformative technology.
The real value in a chat popup, in my view, is the live agent. They can do things and access info not readily available to users.
Most of these companies are still struggling to do BI, let alone AI.
The pendulum is swinging back in favor of these practices as organizations recognize their importance in maintaining data quality, consistency, and governance.
It's like staffing your engineering team with interns and junior-level engineers who are super convincing and often need their code reworked.
Engineers want to engineer. Clanking on a keyboard is a very tactile and cathartic experience for engineers.

2024 seems both exciting and boring for data engineers. It's exciting because data engineering is finally recognized as critical to making data initiatives succeed. It's boring because data engineering is moving toward 'enterprisey' work. These are great things.
A data model is a structured representation that organizes and standardizes data to enable and guide human and machine behavior, inform decision-making, and facilitate actions.
In fact, most data is modeled for machines, not humans. Think of the computer systems you interact with daily - your computer, smartphone, and other smart devices.

Data is growing to a scale where humans have increasing trouble fully reasoning about it without the assistance of machines, and these machines will require better hardware, different types of data processing, and AI.
There is not one true way to fight, and there's no one true approach in data modeling. There are many approaches. Pick what works for your situation.
The discussion of data modeling needs to be expanded away from myopic fixation on any particular approach or use case.
Indie data conferences are vital to the data community because we want a pure experience where we can hang out with peers without vendor noise.
None of us like to be sold to, and we want to hear from our peers in a non-commercial environment instead of a talk that has been curated for months by the vendor's legal department.
As Lynn Bender says, it's a party masquerading as a conference.
Support the people who are supporting the scene of real-world practitioners who have a lot to say, and need a venue to say it in a vendor-agnostic way.
If you disagree, please travel with me in a time machine to 2011, and let's negotiate an on-prem data warehouse and ETL tooling contract with a giant tech vendor of that era.
The MDS made it much easier to work with data, and it also made it much easier to do dumb things more quickly.
I feel like the MDS made it easy for the data industry to go backward, allowing data teams to have a ton of convenient tooling while adding questionable value to their organization.

Data is a thinking person's game. We're finally returning to the discipline and rigor we had in the good old days when we were thoughtful about data instead of just throwing more compute and technology at the problem.
The thing is, the MDS won. It normalized what it's supposed to do - modular, easy-to-use, cloud-based analytical tooling.
Like the dark matter that you can't see, but supposedly comprises most of the universe, dark matter workflows are things you don't see that power the business.
You might be granted a lot of accountability and responsibility but with very little authority.
Until you understand the business objectives and reasons why the data project failed, technology doesn't matter.
Organizations are dynamic and political. In my experience, data projects fail because data teams don't properly address stakeholder needs.
Shine a light on the business stakeholders and make them look like heroes. Make them part of the project's success since you serve them.
It's not always necessary for a data model to be flawless, as long as it facilitates decisions and actions helping move the business in the right direction.
While perfection can benefit a technician's sense of craftsmanship and pride, executives frequently prioritize practicality over perfection.

Practitioners often get caught up in aiming for technical perfection and precision... practitioners should read the room and understand what's expected of them on the spectrum of perfection or practicality.
On another extreme, I've heard people (especially in Europe) strive for things like 6th normal form and the perfect Data Vault or Kimball model, to the point of claiming anything less is garbage.
Data models should be the best for the situation at hand, focusing on outcomes over rigid processes.

Every hype cycle in ML/AI seems to bring about a frenzy of hysteria about how coding will disappear. I remember hearing that deep learning and Big Data would make data warehousing, SQL, and analytics disappear. Data scientists would rule the universe. That didn't happen.

The value of insight shouldn't be predicated on the complexity of its underlying mechanics, that perfect shouldn't be the enemy of good enough and that we should be less dismissive of approaches that don't necessarily fit our perceptions of optimal.
Understand what everyone is thinking about, and then find what is true by understanding where that thinking could be wrong.
Yet another case of why corporate-backed open source is amazing until they decide to change the rules.

There should be some pretty interesting and potentially nasty feedback loops with AI outputs and re-training, especially as developers become more and more disconnected from the code they're responsible for.
If you're delivering value, you're probably busy delivering it instead of talking about it.
I predict that in-person 'meatspace' will become popular again, along with its version of influencers.
This is the US vs Microsoft for the 2020s, and the implications will be massive for the tech industry.
Editing is where the real writing starts.
The act of writing isn't only to blurt onto the page. It's a very personal and selfish act of improving your thinking.
Writing and resting work together to clarify your ideas. I find it's best to write, pause, process, and rewrite.
Outlining is super key for me, as my mind tends to be very chaotic and I've got a lot going on upstairs.
All too often, we see data professionals toiling in the shadows, frustrated they can't break through to an influential career.
Needless to say, there were as many correct answers as there were people in attendance. Everyone's situation differs, and there's not a truly one-size-fits-all approach to analytical data modeling.
Center of Excellence is a four-letter word in some places, and they're often centers of mediocrity, at best.
When van life became 'cool' during the pandemic, I felt a mix of nostalgia and dread...These poor saps bought into some weird social media dream they'd later regret.
There's a massive world of unstructured datasets out there, especially images and video. Most of this data just sits idle on hard drives. What are we doing to unlock meaning and value from this data?
Code generation is hard, and I'm hearing mixed feelings about it from developers. Some like it, some hate it, and others are meh. But, like it or not, code generation is here to stay.
I'm very curious whether generative AI will augment the learning experience or whether it's the road to Idiocracy.
The fact that Andrew Ng and the deeplearning.ai crew are investing a lot of resources into this course should tell you all you need to know.
Too much theory, and you lose hands-on capabilities. Too much hands-on, and you lose sight of the big picture.
I think it will positively impact hundreds of thousands, or possibly millions, of people around the world. This makes it worth it to me.
They're not exactly useless, but they aren't universally useful.
Too often, people try to get buy-in from others from a place of selfishness and lack of empathy. This is almost always a mistake.
Don't use terms like 'normalization' or 'surrogate key' to people who won't know what these mean.
With seemingly every board and CEO fixated on how their company can become AI-driven, educate stakeholders on why good data means AI that serves the business.
If there's ever a time to move on building your data and AI foundation, now is the time (especially before the hype cycle bursts).
Data isn't top of mind for most people, even though organizations are supposedly 'data driven' these days.
Anecdotally, I've been hearing from very senior people (C-level, VP, director) that finding work, especially in tech, is next to impossible.
While LLMs are super impressive for many tasks, they fail at things like Sodoku and Celluar Automata.
This is a good read if you're interested in the power of influencers, and why hype alone is hard for products to sustain.
The choice of programming language is almost always irrelevant to an organization's commercial success.
I often quip Python is the 'second best language at everything.'
You need to upskill your business sense if you're trying to differentiate your product or service based on the underlying programming language or tech stack.
Cool if you're trying to win brownie points with other nerds. Debate about your tech stack all day long. The rest of the world doesn't care.
Slow is relative to the use case, so making a universal claim about speed is a red herring.
You know when your idea is good when people pay real money.
Entrepreneurship isn't for everyone. I'll probably piss off some hustle bros when I say that most people shouldn't bother.
Working for yourself requires such a high level of discomfort and uncertainty that most people will crack under the pressure.

Do you want to look back on your career thinking, 'I was thrilled I spent my time dealing with total assholes at work, just to make sure the data warehouse ran so I can make a bunch of reports and dashboards nobody looks at'?
Stop being so damn transactional. You'll be surprised who might help you when you need it.
Unlike other professions that revere their contributors (think medicine - Hippocrates, Pasteur, etc.), tech and data throw out the legends as soon as it's convenient.
History is important. It helps set the context of the why and what of your profession.
Every innovation you're currently working with happened because people wanted to improve the status quo.
If you understand the building blocks of machine learning, you'll have a much easier time understanding generative AI. It didn't spring out of nowhere.
What happens if the data team disappears tomorrow? If you answer along the lines of 'all hell breaks loose', then keep your data team. If the answer is nothing, then why have a data team?
In Productland, data IS the product. There's a flywheel where more data is used in better ways, which means the product performs better.
As long as data is internally focused, it will suffer from the same politics and shortsighted behavior that's plagued data teams for decades.
Data is a byproduct (sometimes called 'business exhaust') of the enterprise's day-to-day operations.
Based on the conversations (some might say therapy sessions) I have with many people in high-title/rank positions, the lamentation I hear is, 'Is this it? Is this what I worked hard to achieve?'
If this is the only path to success, we might need to rethink what success means.
The corporate executive route was never for me. Yet I'm having a lot of fun and consider myself 'successful.'
You have your path and definition of success. Go that way.
Being one-dimensional in MMA is a recipe for disaster... People working with data in a one-dimensional way are similar to combat sports before the UFC.
Ignore this, and you're like the boxer in UFC 1 wearing a single boxing glove. You're not going to win at the game of data.
AI without good-quality data means you'll do dumb things more quickly.
In general, Productland is the future and Enterpriseland is a dead-end.
Stop obsessing over specific siloed techniques. Get to know the big ideas in data modeling across disciplines and use cases.
I can't imagine the manufacturer advertising the clock as a 'plastic clock.' It's just a clock that happens to include some plastic.
What does a product have to do with data or AI? If a product uses data or AI, it's there to serve the functionality and utility of the product, not the other way around.
I don't believe the end customer cares whether a product is 'AI-powered.' People want products that work.

It's like the tortoise and the hare approach. So I think the modular data stack, the horizontal approach, is definitely the tortoise approach. It requires a lot of open source diplomacy, a lot of coordination across projects, getting people to agree to specialize and then work together toward productive ends.

What world do you want to live in? A vertically integrated one or one where it's horizontally integrated and best of breed? But it's really challenging to deliver cohesive user experiences in the horizontal integration world.
I'd hate to see a world where the AI-Industrial Complex is the gatekeeper to models. It already sucks seeing the big clouds as the tollroads for many companies' tech and data workloads.
The big question is who owns this stack - a handful of big megacorporations or nobody? For innovation and humanity's sake, I very much hope it's the latter.
2024 is the year of f*cking around and finding out how well GenAI works in production.
From what we can tell, most companies are still barely getting BI right. Reports don't match, numbers don't make sense, and the data teams still spend much time cobbling together data.
May as well hand a loaded gun to business stakeholders by passing 'human in the loop' responsibility over to people who are woefully unprepared to suss out when something is blatantly wrong.
It's a wave of 'innovation' that isn't genuinely innovative but leads us down a path of SaaS enshittification.
The user story for most of these features was probably 'As a SaaS vendor, I want to add AI to my product.'
Even more challenging with consulting is that your impact is one-to-one, meaning one consultant and one client.
Since there's no recurring revenue, you're constantly chasing new work, 'feeding the beast,' as they say.
You quickly realize that most companies - big or small, startups or massive - are essentially the same. Same personalities, problems, etc.
I hope all of you can find a way to impact more people in a 1:many way. It's gratifying and creates a flywheel where more impact leads to more opportunities to create an even more significant impact.
Most companies want to do AI. Most are barely doing BI.
AI means you get more work done with fewer humans. Instead of hiring workers to complete a task, I can incorporate ML/AI into a workflow and augment or eliminate the pesky humans.

Here, we have two opposite expectations—one where you constantly reevaluate your skills to execute and deliver daily, and another where you retain your skills in case of rare events.
In both cases, assuming you're employed, you're good enough for your job by definition.
The gap between enthusiasm and real-world implementation is massive.
I'm long-term bullish on AI, but I feel this hype cycle is played out by a crowd of grifters and sameness. Show us the money!

In most companies, there's no incentive to add value. It's easier to pass the buck to the people downstream from you. You play defense and make the next person pay.
The data team is just going through the motions, receiving messy data, doing 'data stuff' like making dashboards and staying tucked away from the business.
It all comes down to stopping the buck-passing and becoming actively involved in the business.
The reality is most of you won't do this. It's hard. And I'll probably write the same thing in five years. Same as it ever was.

Promote your company, product, or service. Nobody - I repeat, nobody - cares.
If you do this to me, I will mercilessly mock you until you cry or leave.

Does income = competence? Absolutely not. I've praised European data practices and often comment that I think Europe often does data 'better' than the US.

In the US, making money is all about moving fast and being as 'productive' as possible to hit your quarterly number. We're not a long-term-oriented business culture.
Scott Galloway says the US is a great place to make money, and Europe is where you should spend it. That's my plan.

You need to be at the level a mentor will find you respectable and capable enough to be worth their time investment. Iron sharpens iron, as the saying goes.
No matter the stage of your career, mentorship is essential. You're going to make a lot of mistakes. Find someone who can guide you in your career and show you the ropes without judgment.
After years of training, playing the game, and 'doing the right thing,' is this what you signed up for? I sense people are rethinking their decisions and realize they can only depend on themselves.

There's your career before and after writing a book. This is very apt. If you want to elevate your profile, write a book. For whatever reason, writing a book puts you in a different class.

I fear we're building a competency debt bubble. Just as we accrue technical debt by cutting corners in code and systems, we are now accruing human debt.
If you can't spot 'implausibly bad' slop, you aren't proficient at AI. You're just a glorified copy-paster who will eventually be replaced by the very tool you're leaning on.
Mindless velocity without comprehension is just a fast track to collective stupidity. Idiocracy was a comedy, not a documentary.
If you stop using your brain, you shouldn't be surprised when it stops working.
Increase revenue, reduce cost, mitigate risk. Yep, that's it. That's the answer. It's not sexy or flashy, and won't win awards.

The people who are 'successful' on social media (whatever that means) usually follow their own game. If you're trying to copy the person next to you, it's harder to stand out, and you'll never be happy.
Education and content is beautiful because it's multiplicative, not zero-sum. In consulting or SaaS, if I win a deal, you lose it, and vice versa.
When AI got hot, everyone (including crypto bros who can barely spell AI) were suddenly AI experts. Now everyone is an ontology expert.
In the end, these status games are mostly meaningless. But we're also wired for status games, and they're necessary in the weird sense they keep society moving along.
The next trillion-dollar opportunity? Owning what the vibe was when it happened.

The market for ideas is saturated. Ideas feel scarce to the person who has them, but they're not scarce to the market.
People don't buy ideas. People buy outcomes. They buy reduced uncertainty, fast delivery, lower risk, and visible wins that make them look good and that they can defend.
If you can't clearly explain why somebody should bet their time, budget, or reputation on your idea, you're not selling. You're presenting and wasting that person's time and yours.
Many people in our industry equate selling with playing the status game of one-upping each other and looking smarter than the other person.
As Warren Buffett said, 'You don't get any extra points for the fact that something's very hard to do.'
Most AI analytics agents fail in predictable ways. They hallucinate tables and joins, infer weird semantics from schemas, and give plausible but incorrect answers.
The key difference is the semantic layer. The agent queries semantic models, not raw schemas. That means it operates inside defined guardrails instead of improvising.
In one test, I asked for data that didn't exist, and it refused rather than hallucinating an answer.
I use AI to do more work… so I can make more money… to buy more tokens… to do more work.
The amount of tacit knowledge locked up in workers' heads is likely an existential impediment to the effective deployment of AI agents in the real world.
If AI makes people, say, 50% more productive, it might make sense to hire more people, not fewer.
Losing those workers doesn't just cut costs; it creates an unsolvable problem for the AI, ultimately requiring more human effort to re-document and re-train.
If you have a growth mindset, AI presents an opportunity to remove toil and get on to solving the problems you might consider impossible today.
Vibes are metadata, not data.
Research shows that meetings accepted with high dread scores have 73% higher rates of 'can we take this offline?' outcomes.
The Monday Effect alone is responsible for 52 false positive vibe alerts per year if you don't normalize for it.
We've found that 5+ consecutive days of Bon Iver, Radiohead, or Elliott Smith is a leading indicator of attrition risk.

The assumption that SaaS was the golden goose was based on the idea that software was hard to build and easy to rent. That dynamic has flipped. Software is now easy to build, and increasingly hard to justify renting.
We are moving from an era of renting generic capabilities to generating specific ones.
If I can describe what your product does in two sentences, AI can probably build it.
Vendors are facing a potential Lilliputian attack of millions of people using AI to chip away at countless vendor flagship offerings from millions of tiny angles.
The tools have caught up with our imaginations, and it turns out our imaginations are insatiable.
Rather than bury the results in a 30-page PDF behind a email form, I built something different: an interactive explorer where you can query the data yourself.
AI is table stakes. 82% of you use AI tools daily or more. Only 3.7% find them unhelpful. But organizational adoption lags way behind.
The bottlenecks aren't technical. Legacy systems top the list (25%), but lack of leadership direction (21%) and poor requirements (19%) are close behind. People problems rival tech debt.
Go find something I missed.
The big theme of 2026 is that unpaid debts of the past carry interest, accruing at payday loan rates.
Disciplined teams will use AI to move faster with quality. Undisciplined teams will use AI to create technical debt faster.
By the end of 2026, 'AI-assisted' will disappear from job descriptions because it will be assumed.
Data engineering in 2026 is less about picking the right tools and more about building the organizational muscle to use them well.
They're not shrinking because of AI or efficiency, but because they never got organizational buy-in. That's tragic.
If the data industry has been teaching and preaching the same way for four decades and the vast majority of practitioners are still struggling, clearly the approach hasn't worked.

Doing the same thing over and over again and expecting different results is the literal definition of insanity. Yet, the data industry keeps insisting that if we just shout louder at people, they'll finally get it.
When someone's hair is on fire, they don't need a lecture on the chemical composition of fire; they need a bucket of water.
If your documentation, training, or book is boring AF, it's going to lose to ChatGPT, Slack pings, endless meetings, and doomscrolling.
Never underestimate how little people know about a topic. Make the material almost absurdly digestible, approachable, and maybe even a little fun.
An industry that spent two decades insisting it could measure everything failed to see this coming, despite generating approximately 47,000 blog posts per quarter about 'the future of data.'
It wasn't. It was three industries wearing a trenchcoat.
The tools designed to democratize data work succeeded — they just democratized it first for machines.

The AI could build a new pipeline from scratch in hours. Understanding why the existing pipeline had a hardcoded filter that excluded all transactions from New Jersey on Tuesdays? That required a human who'd been at the company since 2016.
It turns out that 'tribal knowledge' — the thing every data governance framework promised to eliminate — was the one thing AI couldn't replicate.
The models crossed some threshold where the gap between 'neat demo' and 'this replaces actual work' effectively closed for a huge swath of tasks.

Most engineers don't understand the business either. A huge portion of the data workforce built careers around knowing which YAML config makes Tool A talk to Tool B. That's not business context. That's configuration knowledge.
Whatever you think AI is incapable of today, it's going to surprise you sooner than you expect. Every time I've drawn a line and said, 'It can't do this,' the next model version has erased that line.
If your value proposition is 'I know how to use dbt,' I love dbt, but it's also a means to an end. It's not the job.
The tools are being commoditized. The human judgment layer is what's left.
Life got better when I stopped optimizing for appearance and started optimizing for durability, focus, relationships, and real work.
Subtraction is an adult skill that you learn once you're done saying 'yes' to everything, and realizing the most powerful word in your vocabulary is 'no.' The second most powerful word is 'enough.'
There is a difference between looking productive and producing durable artifacts.
Shallow busyness and business cosplay do not compound. It's a treadmill that makes you run faster and faster, but you're stuck in place.
Success that comes at the cost of your closest relationships is failure.

The problem isn't that Gartner identified a real issue. The problem is the industrial complex that immediately forms around every Gartner proclamation.
The dark irony here? A lot of these teams can barely get their f*cking dashboards working. But AI will do away with dashboards, right?
The Buzzword Industrial Complex will try to convince you otherwise, mostly to keep its flywheel of vendor rankings and hype cycles spinning.
Buying a shiny new toy doesn't atone for past architectural sins.
We're stacking new trends on broken foundations. We haven't even finished the last several 'seasons' of buzzwords, and now we're onto a new one.
If each hype cycle magically solved everything, we wouldn't still be having the exact same conversations about core challenges, like people and processes, that we've been having for decades.
If your work isn't close to production, decisions, or money, you're inevitably cooked.
You're not just building pipelines and data for humans anymore. You're building for bots, too.
If you're waiting for the puck to arrive in your career or in your company, you're probably already too late.
You don't need to become an AI thought leader or an influencer on LinkedIn (please don't). You need to show that AI makes you faster, broader, and more effective in your actual work.
For every task I knock out, there seems to be ten more that magically appear.
AI has changed everything except the hard parts.
If you don't understand what you're building, you're a button pusher, and button pushers are replaceable.
You've been told you don't have time for fundamentals. The data says you don't have time to skip them.
We have a new form of technical debt: code and systems that nobody wrote, created by AI, that nobody fully understands.
The gap between individual tool adoption (near-universal) and organizational AI maturity (still mostly experimental) is where the real work is.

When you have executives essentially viewing employees as token-consumption engines, the humanity gets stripped away. If productivity is measured solely by how many tokens you burn through rather than the judgment you apply, and how much 'stuff' you're cranking out, we are just building a digital sweatshop.
The difference between past sweatshops and the digital one we're about to enter is that we're happily giving the sweatshop feedback on how to do our jobs.
The platitudes that people are going to 'find new things to do, since that's what's always happened' fall flat when the pace of change is at warp speed.
AI saves us time in the short run, but I'm curious whether we'll regret it later. But the token crack pipe is too nice a rush to put down, so we continue taking another hit.
When the hustle demands everything, but the compensation no longer feels worth the existential dread, something has to give.
Building data platforms without understanding the underlying theory is 'vibe engineering.' You operate on vibes rather than a strong theoretical or practical framework.

It's the equivalent of building a house on a steep jungle hillside just because the view is nice, without consulting an engineer first. It works fine...right up until monsoon season hits and your house disappears in a mudslide.
As we move to higher-level orchestrations and 'agentic' workflows, you actually need to understand the innards of your systems more, not less.
If you want to be the yahoo climber learning to climb on the spot, just remember that gravity is indifferent to your opinion on its existence.
As I often quote Bill Inmon: 'The fundamentals are gravity.' You can't escape them.
